Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Cross-Coupling of Acyl Chloride with Racemic alpha-Trifluoromethyl Bromide to Access Chiral alpha-Trifluoromethyl Ketones
Reaction: acyl chlorides (acyl electrophile) + racemic secondary α-CF3-alkyl bromides (radical precursor) → chiral α-CF3 carbonyl compounds

Title: Di(2-picolyl)amines as Modular and Robust Ligands for Nickel-Catalyzed C(sp2)–C(sp3) Cross-Electrophile Coupling
Reaction: Aryl bromides + alkyl bromides → aryl-alkyl cross-coupling products
Ligand Knowledge: A library of 26 DPA ligands was screened via HTE. Electron-donating groups at positions 4 and 5 of the pyridine arms improved outcomes, while electron-withdrawing groups and steric bulk at position 6 decreased reactivity. Reducing the central amine alkyl group size from cyclohexyl (L1) to cyclopropyl (L20) to methyl (L32) consistently improved P/IS ratios across five unique coupling experiments. L20 with cyclopropyl typically outperformed dtbbpy, and the final optimization to L32 (methyl) gave reactivity comparable to L20 for primary alkyl bromides and improved reactivity for secondary alkyl bromides. L32 achieved 90% isolated yield for product 3aa and 68% for 3al, significantly outperforming dtbbpy. DPA ligands showed particular strength for secondary alkyl bromides (cyclopropyl, cyclobutyl), where dtbbpy failed entirely, producing only small amounts of product. For benzylic couplings, dtbbpy remained slightly more efficient than DPA ligands.

Title: Ni-Catalyzed Reductive Coupling of Heteroaryl Bromides with Tertiary Alkyl Halides
Reaction: Bromopyridines + tertiary alkyl bromides → alkylated pyridines with all-carbon quaternary centers
Ligand Knowledge: L1 gave the best result at 69% yield with an excellent 3a/3a′ ratio of 20:1 (Table 1, entry 1). Other monodentate pyridine ligands were inferior: L2 gave 34% (6:1), L3 gave 40% (7:1), L4 gave 58% (6:1), L5 gave 54% (49:1), and L6 gave 18% (10:1). Surprisingly, bidentate bipyridine ligands L7 and 2,2′-bipyridine completely suppressed the reaction, giving only trace product (entries 7–8), and this inhibition persisted even when 100 mol % L1 was present alongside only 10 mol % bpy. Without ligand, only 13% yield was obtained (entry 19), and reducing L1 loading to 50 mol % decreased efficiency (54%, 6:1). The stoichiometric amount of monodentate L1 is required to outcompete coordination of the bromopyridine substrates and pyridine-containing products to the nickel catalyst, preventing catalyst poisoning that bidentate ligands cannot overcome.

Title: One-Pot Chlorination and Cross-Electrophile Coupling of Alcohols with Aryl Chlorides
Optimum Ligand: PyBCamCN (for primary alcohols); ttbtpy (for certain secondary alcohols such as tetrahydropyran-containing substrates)
Reaction: Alkyl alcohols (via in situ deoxychlorination) + aryl chlorides → C(sp2)-C(sp3) cross-coupled products
Ligand Knowledge: The paper does not present a formal ligand screening table in the main text, as PyBCamCN was selected based on prior work. A critical control experiment showed that without PyBCamCN the yield dropped to 2% compared to 86% with the ligand (Table 1, entries 1 and 10), confirming the ligand is essential. For challenging secondary alcohols (3ab, 3ai) that gave low yields under standard PyBCamCN/NiBr2(dme) conditions, switching to ttbtpy with NiCl2(dme) decreased reaction time by 2-fold and improved yields (3ab: 40%, 3ai: 26%). The optimization primarily focused on nickel source, solvent, lithium salt, and halide exchange rather than systematic ligand comparison, so quantitative ligand-to-ligand performance data beyond the no-ligand control are not provided.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Arylation of gem-Bromofluorocyclopropanes To Construct Monofluorinated Cyclopropane Derivatives
Optimum Ligand: L6 (2-(4,5-dihydro-2-oxazolyl)pyridine) for electron-deficient aryl bromides; L7 (5,5′-dimethyl-bipyridine) for electron-neutral/electron-rich aryl bromides
Reaction: gem-Bromofluorocyclopropanes + aryl bromides → monofluorinated cyclopropane derivatives
Ligand Knowledge: Six ligands were initially screened in DMAc: L1 (Me-bpy) gave 10% yield of 3a with 0% ring-opening byproduct 3b, L2 (t-Bu-bpy) gave 13%/3%, L3 (OMe-bpy) gave 3%/0%, L4 gave 32%/0%, L5 gave 6%/0%, and L6 (2-(4,5-dihydro-2-oxazolyl)pyridine) was the best at 39%/9% (Table 1). L6 is a bidentate N,O-ligand whose oxazoline moiety likely provides optimal steric and electronic properties for stabilizing the nickel intermediate while promoting the desired cyclic-selective pathway over ring-opening. Switching the solvent to DMF with L6 improved the yield to 64%/9% (entry 7). For electron-neutral and electron-rich aryl bromides that showed low reactivity under the L6 standard conditions, L7 (5,5′-dimethyl-bipyridine) was found to be crucial, furnishing products 17–20 in 51–64% yields. The anionic effect of the nickel source also influenced regioselectivity: Ni(OAc)2/L6 gave 58% 3a but 26% ring-opening 3b, while NiCl2·DME/L6 favored the cyclic product (75% 3a, 9% 3b).

Title: Ligand-Controlled Preparation of Aryl-Substituted Allenes Enabled by Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Coupling of Secondary Propargylic Carbonates with Aryl Halides
Reaction: Secondary propargylic carbonates + aryl halides → aryl-substituted allenes (C1-arylated)
Ligand Knowledge: Ligand choice critically controlled regioselectivity between allene (C1-arylated) and propargyl (C3-arylated) products. Bipyridine ligands L2 and L3, differing only in methyl group position, showed vastly different regioselectivity—L3 achieved a 9:1 allene:propargyl ratio but only ~20% yield. L8 offered even better regioselectivity due to larger steric hindrance but suffered from lower overall reaction efficiency. L6 (dimethyl-1,10-phenanthroline) was identified as optimal, balancing regioselectivity with 95% isolated yield while nearly completely suppressing propargyl product formation. The steric bulk of the ligand around the nickel center shifts the equilibrium from the more congested C3-η1-propargyl Ni intermediate (P) toward the C1-η2-allenyl Ni intermediate (A), as the crowded P intermediate is disproportionately destabilized by increased ligand steric demand. This represents an unprecedented ligand-controlled regioselective reductive arylation of C3-secondary propargyl electrophiles.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Ipso-Cross-Coupling of Homopropargyl Bromides with Aryl/Vinyl Electrophiles
Reaction: homopropargyl bromides + aryl/vinyl bromides/triflates → functionalized 1,4-biaryl and 1-aryl-4-vinyl internal alkynes
Ligand Knowledge: Among bipyridine-based ligands, 4,4′-dimethyl-2,2′-bipyridine (L4, dmbpy) gave only 19% yield with negligible cyclobutene byproduct. Pyridyl carboxamidine ligands L6–L8 were then tested. Under the initial NiI2/LiI conditions, L6–L8 led to dramatically decreased yield with most starting materials consumed. Upon switching to NiCl2(DME)/Zn without LiI, L7 (PyBCam) delivered 60% yield, further improving to 79% isolated yield at reduced catalyst loading (5 mol% Ni, 8 mol% L7), significantly outperforming all bipyridine-type ligands.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Enantioselective Cross-Coupling of N-Hydroxyphthalimide Esters with Vinyl Bromides
Optimum Ligand: L·NiBr2 (4b) — chiral bisoxazoline–nickel complex from prior Reisman group work
Reaction: N-hydroxyphthalimide (NHP) esters (benzylic/α-alkoxy, derived from carboxylic acids) + (E)-vinyl bromides → enantioenriched C(sp3)–C(sp2) coupling products (alkenylated arenes/alkanes)
Ligand Knowledge: No ligand screening was performed. The same chiral bisoxazoline–nickel complex (L·NiBr2, 4b) from the authors’ previously reported enantioselective reductive coupling of benzyl chlorides with vinyl bromides was used directly; optimization focused on reductant (TDAE), additive (TMSBr), and temperature rather than ligand structure.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Coupling between Benzyl Alcohols and Aryl Halides Assisted by Titanium Co-reductant
Reaction: Benzyl alcohols + aryl halides → diarylmethanes
Ligand Knowledge: Me4Phen (3,4,7,8-tetramethylphenanthroline) and (MeO)2Phen (4,7-dimethoxyphenanthroline) were identified as the best ligands. The preformed NiCl2(Me4Phen)·2H2O catalyst gave 86% yield (entry 1), while NiCl2(PPh3)2 gave only 9% and NiBr2(dme) gave 25% without ligand. Using NiBr2(dme) + Me4Phen gave 77% yield (entry 14) and NiBr2(dme) + (MeO)2Phen gave 81% yield (entry 15), with the isolated NiCl2((MeO)2Phen)·2H2O complex delivering 88% isolated yield (entry 16). The phenanthroline-type ligands clearly outperformed the phosphine (PPh3) ligand and the no-ligand control (0% yield), likely due to their ability to stabilize the nickel intermediates during the radical cross-coupling cycle involving benzyl radicals and arylnickel species.

Title: Ni-Catalyzed Reductive C–O Bond Arylation of Oxalates Derived from α-Hydroxy Esters with Aryl Halides
Reaction: Oxalates derived from α-hydroxy esters + aryl halides → α-aryl esters
Ligand Knowledge: L1 gave 86% isolated yield. L2 gave 71% yield. L3 gave 70% yield with 0% ee. L4 gave only trace product. Without L1, the yield dropped drastically to 17% (Table 1, entries 1, 4–6, 14). The imidazoline-pyridine ligand L1 outperformed all other tested ligands by 15–16 percentage points. Neither NiCl2 (80%) nor NiBr2 (70%) matched NiI2 with L1 (86%). The L1 ligand’s superior performance is attributed to its unique coordination properties that facilitate the Ni-catalyzed C–O bond scission of the oxalate electrophile and subsequent cross-coupling with the aryl halide, while the other ligand frameworks (L2–L4) were ineffective at supporting this transformation.

Title: Mechanical Activation of Zero-Valent Metal Reductants for Nickel-Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Coupling
Reaction: Heteroaryl bromide + alkyl iodide → alkylated heteroarene
Ligand Knowledge: The amidine ligand L1 was critical for the mechanochemical XEC of heteroaryl halides with alkyl iodides. Initial ball-milling conditions adapted from prior aryl/alkyl halide XEC gave only 34% yield for 2-bromopyridine with 1-iodooctane, with bipyridine dimer as a significant byproduct. Optimization revealed that L1, a pyridine-based amidine ligand, led to significantly improved performance, delivering the model target product (5aa) in 72% isolated yield. A metal-to-ligand ratio of 1:2 Ni:L1 provided the greatest yields, outperforming both 1:1 and 1:4 ratios. Control experiments demonstrated that in the absence of both DMA LAG and the ligand, the reaction became significantly poorer for the desired product and lost selectivity versus homocoupling to form 2,2′-bipyridine. For the twisted amide coupling, a separate ligand set was used (details in SI), with optimization focusing on reductant identity (Mn over Zn) and NaCl additive rather than ligand variation.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Inter- and Intramolecular Reductive Cross-Coupling Reactions of Arylbenzylammonium Salts through Highly Site-Selective C−N Bond Cleavage
Reaction: Arylbenzylammonium triflates + aryl bromides → diarylmethanes
Ligand Knowledge: Five nitrogen ligands were screened (Table 1). L1 gave the best yield of 82%. L2 provided 74% yield, and L3 gave 52% yield. L4 and L5 afforded no desired product (0% yield), and the no-ligand control gave only trace product. L1 is therefore essential and uniquely effective for enabling the site-selective C(sp3)–N bond cleavage of arylbenzylammonium salts over C(sp2)–N bond cleavage. The same L1 conditions were also successfully extended to intramolecular coupling of benzyl(bromoaryl)ammonium salts and to benzyl–acyl cross-electrophile coupling with aromatic carboxylic acids (using NiBr2/L1/Mn/NaI/Boc2O).

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Decarboxylative Coupling of Diacyl Peroxides with Aryl/Vinyl Halides
Reaction: Alkyl diacyl peroxides (in situ alkyl iodides) + aryl/vinyl halides → C(sp3)–C(sp2) cross-coupling products
Ligand Knowledge: The paper uses only ligand L1 (5 mol %) with NiCl2(dme) as the catalyst. No ligand comparison data is provided in the main text; the optimization details are deferred to the Supporting Information (ref 16). A no-ligand control was not reported for the cross-coupling, but CuI was found to competitively coordinate with L1 and inhibit the coupling when alkyl iodides were used directly rather than in situ generated from diacyl peroxides, suggesting L1 is essential for Ni catalysis. The mCBA byproduct from diacyl peroxide decomposition serves as a noninnocent ligand to Cu, sparing L1 for Ni complexation.

Title: Enantioconvergent Cross-Electrophile Coupling of 2-Aryloxetanes with Aryl and Vinyl Halides, or Anhydrides
Optimum Ligand: L6 (chiral bis(imidazoline)) for aryl/vinyl halides; L7 ((L7)NiBr2 complex) for anhydrides
Reaction: 2-Aryloxetanes + aryl/vinyl halides or anhydrides → γ,γ-disubstituted alcohols or esters
Ligand Knowledge: Common bis(oxazoline) ligands L1–L4 were completely ineffective for this enantioselective ring-opening arylation (0% yield for L1–L3, 22% yield and −51% ee for L4). Chiral bis(imidazoline) ligands proved far superior: L5 gave 63% yield with 90% ee, while the optimal L6 afforded 80% yield with 94% ee. The increased electron-donating properties of bis(imidazoline) ligands were reasoned to facilitate oxidative addition of aryl bromides to the nickel catalyst. For anhydride coupling, the chiral (L7)NiBr2 precomplex was required instead of in situ L6/Ni combination, providing 71% yield with 90% ee at 10 °C. The enantioselectivity is determined by the stereochemistry of the ligand rather than the oxetane starting material.

Title: Decarboxylative and Deformylative Alkyl-Alkyl Cross-Coupling Enabled by Nickel/Photoredox Catalysis
Reaction: Redox-active esters (from carboxylic acids) + 4-alkyl-dihydropyridines (from aldehydes) → C(sp3)-C(sp3) coupled products
Ligand Knowledge: The addition of exogenous multidentate nitrogen ligands L1–L4 (commonly used in Ni-catalyzed XEC, 20 mol%) was found to be detrimental, decreasing the yield from 65% to 16–21%. The tridentate ligand KTp* (20 mol%) also did not improve the reaction, giving 62% yield compared to 65% without ligand. The reaction operates optimally with Ni(acac)2 as the anion-coordinated catalyst without any supporting ligand. The authors hypothesize that the absence of a supporting ligand may result in Ni(II) species being difficult to reduce to low-valent organonickel species, but the nickel center is still essential for C–C bond formation as no product forms without Ni salt, photocatalyst, or light.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Asymmetric Reductive Arylation of α-Amino-CF3 Acetates: A Strategy for the Enantioselective Synthesis of α-Trifluoromethyl Amine Derivatives
Reaction: α-CF3 amino acetates + (hetero)aryl halides → chiral α-trifluoromethyl amine derivatives (CF3AMA)
Ligand Knowledge: After extensive screening of chiral ligands, L1 (a chiral diimidazoline) proved optimal, delivering 80% isolated yield with 95:5 er. Replacement of L1 with other chiral diimidazoline ligands L2–L6 did not result in better outcomes, demonstrating the superior performance of L1 in both stereocontrol and catalytic efficiency (entries 8–12 of Table 1). The stereoselectivity is also highly dependent on the amino-protecting group, with pyrrole-2-carbonyl providing the best results over N-methylpyrrole, benzopyrrole, furanyl, and 2,6-dimethylphenyl variants. DFT studies revealed that Ni(III) to Ni(I) reductive elimination is the rate-determining step responsible for the observed enantioselectivity.

Title: Harnessing a Tripyridyl Ligand in Zirconaaziridine-Mediated Ni-Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Couplings
Reaction: Cyclohexyl iodide + (hetero)aryl iodide → diastereoselective 1,4-disubstituted cycloalkane
Ligand Knowledge: Systematic screening of nitrogen-based ligands revealed that the tripyridyl ligand L10 provided unmatched stereocontrol for this zirconaaziridine-mediated XEC. Phenanthroline derivatives L1 and L2 afforded acceptable yields but only moderate diastereoselectivity favoring the trans-isomer. Electronic modification of bipyridyl ligands L3 and L4 had minimal influence on stereochemical outcome. Ortho-substituted phenanthroline ligands L5 and L6 significantly impaired coupling efficiency. Pyridine-oxazoline ligands L7–L9 substantially improved performance, delivering trans-configured product with d.r. exceeding 82:18, but additional chiral pyridine/oxazoline analogues showed no improvement. L10 (tripyridyl) outperformed all bidentate ligands, furnishing the desired product in 71% yield with a 92:8 diastereomeric ratio at 0.40 M concentration. The common terpyridine ligand L11 resulted in diminished yield, underscoring that the specific tripyridyl framework of L10, rather than simple tridentate coordination, is responsible for the superior diastereocontrol. The tridentate L10 ligand likely enforces a specific coordination geometry that governs the stereochemical course of radical capture at the nickel center.

Title: Ligand-Controlled Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Relay Cross-Coupling of Alkyl Bromides and Aryl Bromides
Reaction: Alkyl bromide + aryl bromide → 1,1-diarylalkane
Ligand Knowledge: The ligand uniquely dictated the regioselectivity of this reductive relay cross-coupling between 1-bromo-3-phenylpropane and bromobenzene. Bipyridines (L1, L2, L3) and 1,10-phenanthrolines (L4, L5, L6) selectively gave the ipso-coupling product 3a, with 5,5′-dimethylbipyridine (L3) affording the best ipso result at 82% isolated yield with exclusive regioselectivity. In contrast, 6,6′-dimethylbipyridine (L7) and 2,9-dimethyl-1,10-phenanthrolines (L8, L9) switched the selectivity to the benzylic phenylation product 4a. Among these, bathocuproine (L9) proved most efficient, delivering 85% isolated yield of 4a with a 28:1 regioisomeric ratio on a 0.5 mmol scale. The steric profile of the ligand controls the two competing pathways: L3-type ligands favor direct reductive elimination from the Ni(III) intermediate (pathway A, ipso-product), while bathocuproine (L9) promotes multiple rapid and reversible beta-hydride elimination and migratory insertion steps (pathway B, chain-walking) to reach the thermodynamically more stable benzylic-Ni(III) intermediate before reductive elimination.

Title: Ni-Catalyzed Reductive Coupling of Alkyl Acids with Unactivated Tertiary Alkyl and Glycosyl Halides
Optimum Ligand: 4b for tertiary alkyl bromides + alkyl acids (method A); 3a for secondary alkyl iodides + alkyl acids (method B); 4a for secondary alkyl bromides + alkyl acids (method C)
Reaction: Alkyl acids + tertiary alkyl bromides or glycosyl bromides → ketones or C-acyl glycosides
Ligand Knowledge: The optimal ligand is highly context-dependent. For tertiary alkyl bromides with alkyl acids (method A), ligand 4b gave 82% yield, dramatically outperforming 4a (39%), 4c (46%), 5a (<10%), 6a (<10%), 3a (16%), and 3b (7%). Notably, ligand performance is sensitive to MgCl2 loading: reducing MgCl2 from 1.5 to 1.0 equiv decreased yield with 4a (47% to 39%) but increased yield with 4b (19% to 65%), leading to 4b being selected for method A. For secondary alkyl iodides (method B), 3a was optimal (95% yield). For secondary alkyl bromides (method C), 4a was optimal (90% yield in DMF/THF). For glycosyl bromide couplings, method B (3a) and C/C1 (4a) were effective while method A (4b) was not.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Coupling of Aryl Chlorides with Primary Alkyl Chlorides
Reaction: Aryl chloride + primary alkyl chloride → aryl-alkyl cross-coupled product
Ligand Knowledge: PyBCamCN was the only ligand that achieved high-yielding and selective cross-coupling of aryl chlorides with alkyl chlorides. With chloride as the counterion and no additive, PyBCamCN gave 46% cross-coupled product (3a) with only 1% aryl dimer (4) and 7% alkyl dimer (5). In stark contrast, bipyridine (bpy) with chloride gave only 2% cross-product but 48% aryl dimer, failing to consume the alkyl chloride. Pyridine-2-carboxamidine (PyCam) with chloride gave 11% cross-product but 19% hydrodehalogenated arene, without consuming the alkyl chloride. Terpyridine (tpy) converted alkyl chloride to dimeric (10%) and hydrodehalogenated (25%) products without consuming aryl chloride. The closely related PyBCam gave only 4% cross-product with bromide. PyBCamCN with iodide additive achieved the best result at 87% yield (82% isolated) of cross-coupled product with 0% dimer byproducts. PyBCamCN’s tridentate coordination and electron-withdrawing cyano groups enable it to activate the stronger C–Cl bonds while maintaining high chemoselectivity.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Enantioselective Coupling of Acid Chlorides with alpha-Bromobenzoates: An Asymmetric Acyloin Synthesis
Optimum Ligand: L4 (chiral bis(oxazoline) with four-stereocentered scaffold and sterically hindered linkage)
Reaction: acid chlorides (acyl donor) + α-bromobenzoates (ketyl radical donor, generated in situ from aldehydes) → enantioenriched protected α-hydroxy ketones (acyloins)
Ligand Knowledge: L4 gave 68% yield, 95% ee; other chiral bis(oxazoline) ligands L1-L3 led to inferior yields and enantiomeric excesses. The four-stereocentered bisoxazoline scaffold with a sterically hindered linkage in L4 provides favorable enantioselective control during reductive elimination from the acyl-Ni intermediate.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Enantioselective Electrochemical Reductive Cross-Coupling of Aryl Aziridines with Alkenyl Bromides
Optimum Ligand: L1 (10.1021/jacs.2c12869); L9 for 2-bromo-1H-indene and aryl bromide couplings
Reaction: aryl aziridines (C(sp3) electrophile) + alkenyl bromides (C(sp2) electrophile) → beta-aryl homoallylic amines
Ligand Knowledge: L1 (cyclopropyl-linked indanyl-bis(oxazoline)) gave 70-75% yield, 96:4 er. Different central linkers (L2-L4) gave lower reactivity/ee. Pyridine-oxazoline L5 gave low er. Bioxazoline and bisimidazoline ligands (L6-L9) gave lower yields with moderate ee. L9 required for coupling with 2-bromo-1H-indene and aryl bromides under modified conditions.

Title: Computationally Guided Ligand Discovery from Compound Libraries and Discovery of a New Class of Ligands for Ni-Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Coupling of Challenging Quinoline Halides
Reaction: alpha-bromoquinolines and alpha-bromoisoquinolines + alkyl bromides → C(sp2)-C(sp3) coupled products
Ligand Knowledge: L6, a pyridine-oxime ligand discovered through computationally guided screening (Mulliken charge-yield correlation), outperformed all tested alternatives in couplings of challenging alpha-haloazines. For every reaction tested, PyOxime ligands outperformed both the best PyCam ligands (E1, E2) and bpy-based ligands. On 100 mg scale couplings of quinoline and isoquinoline electrophiles, pyridine-oxime ligands gave synthetically useful yields in all four cases, representing some of the highest C(sp2)-C(sp3) XEC yields reported for 2-haloazines. Computational redox potentials indicate more favorable Ni redox cycling for PyOxime than bpy-supported Ni.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Coupling of Aryl Triflates with Alkyl Halides: Mechanism-Informed Design of More General Conditions
Optimum Ligand: phen (phenanthroline) for electron-deficient aryl triflates; PyBCamCN (pyridine-2,6-bis(N-cyanocarboxamidine), L9) for electron-neutral/rich aryl triflates and alkyl chlorides NiCl2(dme)
Reaction: aryl triflates + alkyl bromides/chlorides → alkylated arenes (C(sp2)-C(sp3) cross-coupled products)
Ligand Knowledge: For electron-deficient aryl triflates, phenanthroline was best (90% yield vs bathophenanthroline 74%, bpy 57%, dtbbpy 15%, dmbpy 8%). However, phen-ligated nickel undergoes off-cycle transmetalation to form unproductive aryl zinc reagents with electron-rich aryl triflates (18% yield). PyBCamCN avoids this by binding in a planar tridentate fashion, suppressing aryl zinc formation and delivering 52% yield with electron-rich 4-methoxyphenyl triflate (vs tpy 9%, BpyCam 7%, PyBCam 41%). PyBCamCN was also optimal for alkyl chlorides. Average yield across 38 examples was 61 +/- 14%.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Asymmetric Reductive Cross-Coupling between Heteroaryl Iodides and alpha-Chloronitriles
Reaction: alpha-chloronitriles + heteroaryl iodides → alpha,alpha-disubstituted nitriles
Ligand Knowledge: DMMB-PHOX (L6) was identified as optimal after systematic screening, providing 78% yield and 84% ee. Chiral BOX L1 gave 0% yield (hydrodehalogenation dominated); BiOX L2 gave 17% yield and 9% ee; PHOX L3 gave 25% yield and 83% ee; another PHOX L4 gave 0% yield; BnPHOX L5 gave 52% yield and 77% ee. No-ligand controls gave 4% yield with 0% ee. More electron-rich ligands (PHOX family) accelerate oxidative addition of the heteroaryl iodide to LNi(0) relative to hydrodehalogenation of the alpha-chloronitrile. Many products could be recrystallized to >95% ee.

Title: A Unified Approach to Chiral alpha-Aryl Ketones and Aldehydes via Ni-Catalyzed Asymmetric Reductive Cross-Coupling
Optimum Ligand: cy-BiOX L1 (with iPr-BiOX L2 and sterically demanding L6 as variants for specific substrates)
Reaction: alpha-iodo acetals/ketals + (hetero)aryl halides (bromides/iodides) → protected chiral tertiary alpha-aryl ketones and aldehydes
Ligand Knowledge: The cy-BiOX L1 ligand provides a bidentate chiral coordination environment enabling sterics-dictated enantiocontrol of unactivated acetal/ketal alpha-carbon radical intermediates. DFT calculations reveal that alkyl radical addition to the Ar-(L)NiIIX intermediate is both the enantioselectivity-determining and turnover-limiting step. The standard conditions with L1 gave 88% yield and 93% ee for the model reaction; for substrates with linear alkyl chains, iPr-BiOX L2 improved ee but lowered yield, while the more sterically demanding L6 proved superior for beta-methyl, gamma-hydroxyl, and beta-ester substituted substrates.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Enantioconvergent Cross-Electrophile Coupling of Benzylic Alcohols with Alkenyl Electrophiles
Reaction: benzylic alcohols (in situ activated as benzyl oxalates) + alkenyl bromides → enantioenriched alkenes bearing allylic stereogenic centers
Ligand Knowledge: Bis(oxazoline) and pyridyloxazoline ligands (L1–L8), (S)-Ph-Phox (L9), and (S,Sp)-iPr-Phosferrox (L10) were screened. L3 and L4 gave good yields but only moderate enantioselectivity. Chiral bis(imidazoline) ligands (L11–L17) provided superior enantiocontrol attributed to their increased steric bulk, which enhances enantiofacial differentiation during the radical capture step of the benzyl radical onto the alkenyl–Ni(II) intermediate. L16 delivered the best combination of yield (84%) and ee (93%).

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Asymmetric Reductive Cross-Coupling To Access 1,1-Diarylalkanes
Optimum Ligand: L1 (4-heptyl-BiOX), a newly developed chiral bioxazoline ligand
Reaction: (hetero)aryl iodides + secondary benzylic chlorides → 1,1-diarylalkanes
Ligand Knowledge: Extensive screening of chiral bidentate ligands was performed. L1 (4-heptyl-BiOX) gave 84% yield and 90% ee; L2 (iPr-BiOX) gave 22% yield and 68% ee; L3 gave 64% yield and 75% ee; L4 gave 74% yield and 80% ee; L5 (Bn-BiOX) gave only 4% yield and 60% ee; L6 (Fu-type) gave 31% yield and 86% ee. Increasing the alkyl chain length on the BiOX scaffold improved both yield and enantioselectivity by optimally balancing the steric environment at nickel. On 2.0 mmol scale, 3a was obtained in 63% yield and 91% ee. Tolerates pyridines, pyrimidines, indoles, and various benzylic chlorides.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Enantioselective Reductive Cross-Coupling of Styrenyl Aziridines
Optimum Ligand: For racemic coupling: 2,6-bis(N-pyrazolyl)pyridine (bpp) at; for enantioselective coupling: (S)-4-heptyl bis(oxazoline) ligand L7
Reaction: styrenyl aziridines (racemic, tosyl-protected) + aryl/heteroaryl iodides → 2-arylphenethylamines
Ligand Knowledge: For the racemic reaction, bpp delivered the cross-coupled product in 80% yield, dramatically outperforming bipyridine (35%), QuinOx (40%), PyBox (<5%), and BiOx (42%), all at 12 mol%. Without any ligand, only trace product (<5%) was detected. For the enantioselective variant, L7 (4-heptyl BiOx) achieved up to 94% ee and 90% ee under optimized conditions at -10 C in THF, with NaI and catalytic TMSCl as additives. Multivariate analysis of 17 BiOx ligands revealed that steric minimum width (B1), NBO charge of oxazoline oxygens (electronic), and ligand polarizability (noncovalent interactions) govern enantioselectivity.

Title: Synthesis of Enantioenriched Allylic Silanes via Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Cross-Coupling
Reaction: (chlorobenzyl)silanes (C(sp3) electrophile) + vinyl bromides (C(sp2) electrophile) → chiral allylic silanes
Ligand Knowledge: L1 gave 69% yield, 97% ee (with 2.0 equiv vinyl bromide and 5 mol% CoPc co-catalyst); L2 gave 14% yield, 41% ee; L3 gave 49% yield, -90% ee (opposite configuration). L1 dramatically outperforms in both yield and enantioselectivity. Preformed L1-NiCl2 complex gave comparable results (70% yield, 97% ee).

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Cyclization of Alkyl Dihalides
Reaction: N-tethered dihaloalkanes → cyclized products (five-, six-, and seven-membered rings)
Ligand Knowledge: Ligand 4b enables the reductive intramolecular cyclization of dihaloalkanes via a proposed c-alkyl–Ni(III) intermediate formed through sequential oxidative additions and single-electron reductions. Five-membered rings were formed in up to 93% yield, six-membered rings up to 66%, and a seven-membered ring in 38% yield, with secondary alkyl halides generally giving higher cyclization efficiency than primary/primary combinations.

Title: New ligands for nickel catalysis from diverse pharmaceutical heterocycle libraries
Reaction: aryl halides (including 2-substituted bromoarenes) + alkyl halides → alkyl-aryl coupled products; heteroaryl bromides + alkyl halides → heteroaryl-alkyl coupled products
Ligand Knowledge: Pyridyl-2-carboxamidine A8 consistently outperformed the standard ligand dmbpy (C1) across all substrate classes. For 2-bromo-N-Boc-aniline, A8 gave 63% yield vs 1% for C1; for 2-bromophenylboronic acid, A8 gave 62% vs 34% for C1. Electron-donating groups at the 4 and 5 positions of the pyridine ring (as in A8) improved yields. For heteroaryl couplings, the tridentate 2,6-pyridinedicarboxamidine A15 was uniquely effective (>70% assay yield), whereas terpyridine gave only 10%, demonstrating that the carboxamidine core is responsible for superior selectivity over homocoupling.

Title: Asymmetric Construction of Allylic Stereogenic Carbon Center Featuring a Trifluoromethyl Group via Enantioselective Reductive Fluoroalkylation
Optimum Ligand: L9 (bis(oxazoline) with gem-disubstituted backbone and 3,5-dimethylphenyl oxazoline substituents)
Reaction: vinyl iodides (C(sp2) electrophile) + trifluoromethylated alkyl bromides (C(sp3) electrophile) → α-trifluoromethylated allylic alkanes
Ligand Knowledge: L1 (simple bis(oxazoline)) gave 52% yield, 64% ee; L3 (tridentate) gave 0% yield. Gem-disubstituted L4-L6 improved ee to 77-88%. L7-L8 raised ee to 90-93% and yield to 51%. L9 with 3,5-dimethylphenyl groups (vs phenyl in L8) gave optimal 90% yield, 95% ee. Increasing steric bulk on oxazoline substituents from phenyl (L8) to 3,5-dimethylphenyl (L9) improves partner differentiation.

Title: Ni-electrocatalytic Csp3–Csp3 doubly decarboxylative coupling
Reaction: carboxylic acid RAEs (primary/secondary) + carboxylic acid RAEs (primary/secondary) → C(sp3)–C(sp3) coupled products (1°–1°, 1°–2°, and selected 1°–3°)
Ligand Knowledge: L4 (an OMe-substituted tridentate pybox ligand) was identified as optimal through extensive screening, giving 68% heterocoupling yield. Switching from bidentate L6 (52% homo, 27% hetero) to tridentate terpyridine L1 (71% homo, 50% hetero) increased reactivity, then to pybox L2 further enhanced radical capture (55% hetero) but generated excessive alkane byproduct (39%). The subtle electronic modulation of L2 to L4 suppressed alkane formation from ~39% to just 1–7% while maintaining high heterocoupling efficiency (68%), making L4 the best performer.

Title: Ni-catalysed reductive cross-coupling of aryl halides with allylic acetates
Reaction: aryl halides + substituted allylic acetates → aryl-allyl cross-coupling products
Ligand Knowledge: L3a (pyridinylimidazole) is optimal for substituted allylic acetates, where dtbbpy dropped from 87% (unsubstituted) to 20% (substituted). The imidazole N provides a distinct coordination environment compared to bipyridine, enabling coupling with sterically hindered allylic substrates.

Title: Alkyl–aryl ketone synthesis via nickel-catalyzed reductive coupling of alkyl halides with aryl acids and anhydrides
Reaction: alkyl bromides + aryl acids/anhydrides → alkyl-aryl ketones; 1-glycosyl bromides + aryl anhydrides → acyl C-glycosides
Ligand Knowledge: Ligand 4b delivered 88% yield for the model coupling of 4-bromo-1-tosylpiperidine with benzoic acid, far surpassing bipyridine-type ligands 3a–c (22–27%), the related phenanthroline 4a (47%) and 4c (53%), and 5a (71%). For glycosyl bromide coupling with benzoic anhydride, 4a became optimal (92% yield, 2.7:1 alpha/beta). The phenanthroline scaffold of 4b provides the optimal bite angle and electronic properties to support the Ni-catalyzed reductive acylation cycle, enabling coupling with only 1.5 equiv of aryl acid and the practical Ni(acac)2 precatalyst.

Title: Ni-Catalyzed Reductive Coupling of alpha-Halocarbonyl Derivatives with Vinyl Bromides
Optimum Ligand: Pyridine [Py] for aryl-conjugated vinyl bromides; Pyridine [Py] + 2,2'-bipyridine [Bipy] for alkyl-substituted vinyl bromides
Reaction: α-halocarbonyl derivatives (alkyl radical precursor) + vinyl bromides → beta,γ-unsaturated esters and amides
Ligand Knowledge: Two distinct ligand regimes: for aryl-conjugated vinyl bromides, pyridine alone gave 88%; adding 2,2'-bipyridine eroded yield to 70%. For alkyl-substituted vinyl bromides, pyridine alone gave 10%, but adding 2,2'-bipyridine (30 mol%) boosted yield to 86%. The bidentate bipyridine is essential for more challenging alkyl-substituted vinyl bromides to stabilize the Ni intermediate and facilitate oxidative addition.

Title: Nickel-catalyzed methylation of aryl halides/tosylates with methyl tosylate
Reaction: aryl bromides + methyl tosylate → methylarenes; aryl tosylates + methyl tosylate → methylarenes
Ligand Knowledge: L1a with NiI2 delivered the methylation product in 93% isolated yield (method A). Other ligands were significantly inferior: L1b gave 80%, L1c gave 85%, L2 and L4 gave no detectable product, and L3 gave only 56%. Removing L1a entirely produced no product. For aryl tosylates (method B), NiCl2-glyme with dppf and LiCl gave 75% yield, whereas method A gave only 40% on tosylate substrates.

Title: Reductive coupling of benzyl oxalates with highly functionalized alkyl bromides by nickel catalysis
Optimum Ligand: P(4-CF3Ph)3 (for naphthyl substrates); dppb + 3-CF3-Py + MgBr2 (for regular benzyl substrates)
Reaction: benzyl oxalates (including 2-naphthylmethyl) + alkyl bromides → ArCH2-R products
Ligand Knowledge: P(4-CF3Ph)3, with electron-withdrawing 4-CF3 substituents, was the most effective phosphine ligand for promoting reductive cross-coupling via benzylic C–O (oxalate) bond cleavage. The method accommodated a wide range of functionalized alkyl bromides (primary and secondary), with gram-scale coupling giving 73–82% yield. No erosion in enantioselectivity was observed for chiral substrates, indicating a radical-based mechanism with stereoretention at non-radical-bearing stereocenters.

Title: Dual nickel- and photoredox-catalyzed reductive cross-coupling of aryl vinyl halides and unactivated tertiary alkyl bromides
Reaction: aryl vinyl bromides/iodides + unactivated tertiary alkyl bromides → vinyl arenes bearing all-carbon quaternary centers (E-isomers)
Ligand Knowledge: NHC A1 is essential for achieving the optimal 73% yield (E/Z > 420:1); omission of A1 caused the yield to plummet to 40%, while other imidazole salts and nitrogen-containing ligands resulted in dramatically decreased yields. The Ni source is Ni(TMHD)2, which operates in conjunction with the NHC additive, Ir photocatalyst, TMEDA reductant, and MgCl2 under blue LED irradiation. The NHC ligand plays a unique structural or electronic role beyond typical N-donor ligands in this dual catalytic system.

Title: Nickel-catalyzed migratory alkyl–alkyl cross-coupling reaction
Reaction: primary alkyl bromides + secondary alkyl bromides → benzylic alkylation products (via migratory cross-coupling)
Ligand Knowledge: The PyrOx ligand L1 was essential for achieving the migratory pathway, delivering the benzylic product in 70% isolated yield with excellent 27:1 regioselectivity. Other nitrogen-based ligands failed dramatically: PyrOx L2 gave 52% yield with 10:1 rr, 6,6'-dimethyl-2,2'-bipyridine (L3) gave 4%, bathocuproine (L4) gave trace. Critically, when 2,2'-bipyridine or 1,10-phenanthroline was used, the non-migratory product formed instead, demonstrating that L1's steric profile uniquely enables the nickel chain-walking migratory mechanism.

Title: Nickel-catalyzed reductive monofluoroalkylation of alkyl tosylate with bromofluoromethane to primary alkyl fluoride
Reaction: alkyl tosylates + bromofluoromethane → primary alkyl fluorides
Ligand Knowledge: The pyridine-imidazoline L1 outperformed all other nitrogen- and phosphine-based ligands tested, including bpy (8% yield), dtbpy (10%), and other pyridine-imidazoline analogues L2–L5 (0–20% yield). With L1, the yield progressively improved from 23% to 87% upon optimization of the base (K2CO3), nickel source (NiCl2-DME), solvent (DMAc), and stoichiometry. L1's ability to support the radical chain mechanism—generating both monofluoromethyl and alkyl radicals at nickel—is critical, as no product formed in its absence.

Title: Nickel-catalyzed cross-electrophile coupling of aryl bromides and cyclic secondary alkyl bromides with spiro-bidentate-pyox ligands
Reaction: aryl bromides + cyclic secondary alkyl bromides → aryl-secondary alkyl C(sp2)–C(sp3) coupled products
Ligand Knowledge: The spiro-bidentate-pyox L1a gave 92% yield, outperforming related spiro-pyox ligands L1b–d (12–34%), classical pyox ligands L2a–b (32–37%), 1,10-phenanthroline L4 (44%), dtbbpy L5 (50%), and tridentate pybox ligands L6–L7 (7–12%). The spirocyclic framework modulates metal–ligand coordination and steric hindrance, while the electron-deficient pyridine nitrogen promotes reductive elimination and the electron-rich oxazoline nitrogen promotes oxidative addition, achieving a balanced catalytic cycle for challenging secondary alkyl bromide coupling.

Title: Nickel-catalyzed asymmetric reductive arylation of alpha-chlorosulfones with aryl halides
Optimum Ligand: L1 (chiral bis(oxazoline) with rigid cyclopropyl linker)
Reaction: aryl/heteroaryl halides + racemic alpha-chlorosulfones → enantioenriched alpha,alpha-disubstituted sulfones
Ligand Knowledge: L1 provided 76% yield and 91% ee (using preformed L1-NiBr2 complex). L2 (another bisoxazoline with tricyclic fused rings) was comparable (71% yield, 90% ee), while L3-L6 gave significantly worse results (28-65% yields, 40-88% ee). The rigid cyclopropyl linker and tricyclic fused rings of L1 confer optimal steric bulk and rigidity for enantioselective control over the radical-Ni cross-coupling.

Title: Nickel-catalysed cross-electrophile coupling of aryl bromides and primary alkyl bromides
Optimum Ligand: L1d (spiro-bidentate-pyox with electron-withdrawing group at C5 of the pyridine ring)
Reaction: aryl bromides + primary alkyl bromides → primary alkylated arenes
Ligand Knowledge: L1d gave 72% yield under initial screening, superior to L1a (67%), L1b (phenyl-substituted, 35%), L1c (electron-donating, 25%), L2 (trace), L3 (10%), L4 (55%), and L5 (35%). With optimized conditions (DMPU solvent, LiBr additive), the yield improved to 91%. The spiro-bidentate-pyox scaffold with a C5 electron-withdrawing substituent provides optimal electronic and steric properties for stabilizing the Ni catalytic cycle in primary alkyl-aryl cross-coupling.

Title: Asymmetric synthesis of aryl/vinyl alkyl carbinol esters via Ni-catalyzed reductive arylation/vinylation of 1-chloro-1-alkanol esters
Reaction: racemic 1-chloro-1-alkanol esters + aryl/vinyl halides or triflates → enantioenriched aryl/vinyl alkyl carbinol esters

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Cross-Coupling of Vinyl Bromides with Unactivated Alkyl Halides
Reaction: vinyl bromides + unactivated alkyl halides → vinylated alkanes

Title: Reductive deaminative cross-coupling of alkyl bistriflimides enabled by electrocatalysis
Reaction: alkyl bistriflimides + (hetero)aryl bromides/iodides → alkylated (hetero)arenes; alkyl bistriflimides + alkyl bistriflimides → C(sp3)-C(sp3) coupled products
Ligand Knowledge: Bipyridine and terpyridine led predominantly to alkyl bistriflimide homocoupling with negligible heterocoupling, while PyBox afforded only mediocre yield, underscoring that the tridentate N-donor framework of bpp ligand is essential for controlling selectivity toward cross-coupling under electrochemical conditions.

Title: A decarbonylative approach to alkylnickel intermediates and C(sp3)-C(sp3) bond formation
Reaction: aliphatic acid 2-pyridyl esters + alkyl iodides → C(sp3)-C(sp3) coupled products (via decarbonylation)
Ligand Knowledge: Mebpp gave 82% yield of decarbonylated product with only 3% ketone byproduct. In dramatic contrast, bipyridine resulted primarily in ketone formation due to CO poisoning, forming stable (bpy)Ni(CO)2. The rigid tridentate ligand geometry enhancing CO release from the 18e complex.

Title: General and Modular Access to Enantioenriched alpha-Trifluoromethyl Ketones via Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Trifluoroalkylation
Reaction: acid chlorides (acyl donor) + racemic α-CF3 alkyl bromides (C(sp3) electrophile) → enantioenriched α-CF3 ketones
Ligand Knowledge: Increasing ortho-steric bulk on gem-diaryl groups from H to 2-iPr progressively improved enantiomeric ratio.

Title: Reductive Cross-Coupling of Aryl Halides with Tertiary Alkyl Bromides
Optimum Ligand: 4,4′-di-tert-butyl-2,2′-bipyridine [4,4'-tBu-bpy] for most substrates; 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-3,5-heptadecane [TMHD, dicarbonyl ligand] for tertiary alkyl bromides
Reaction: aryl halides + tertiary alkyl bromides → aryl-tertiary alkyl products

Title: One-Shot Radical Cross Coupling Between Benzyl Alcohols and Alkenyl Halides Using Ni/Ti/Mn System
Optimum Ligand: 3,4,7,8-tetramethyl-1,10-phenanthroline [TMPhen], as preformed [NiCl2(TMPhen)(H2O)2]; 4,7-dimethoxy-1,10-phenanthroline [4,7-OCH3-phen] as preformed NiCl2(4,7-OCH3-phen) complex for alkenyl iodides.
Reaction: benzyl alcohols (radical precursor via Ti-mediated C-O homolysis) + alkenyl bromides (alkenyl electrophile) → allylbenzene derivatives
Ligand Knowledge: For sterically demanding tri-substituted alkenes, increasing catalyst loading increase the yield, indicating ligand/metal complex concentration is critical for trapping hindered radicals.

Title: Electrochemical Reductive Cross-Coupling of Aryl Halides with Alkyl Halides
Reaction: aryl halides + alkyl halides → aryl-alkyl cross-coupling products

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Cross-Coupling of Heteroaryl Halides with Alkyl Halides
Optimum Ligand: N-cyanopicolinimidamide [PyCamCN] for 4-pyridyl; 4,4′-di-tert-butyl-2,2′-bipyridine [4,4'-tBu-bpy] for simple aryl; picolinimidamide [PyCamH] for 3-bromopyridine
Reaction: heteroaryl halides + alkyl halides → heteroaryl-alkyl products

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Cross-Coupling of Aryl Halides with Alkyl Halides Using B2Pin2 as Reductant
Reaction: aryl halides + alkyl halides → aryl-alkyl cross-coupling products
Ligand Knowledge: 4,4'-di-tert-butyl-2,2'-bipyridine ligand is optimal; electron-withdrawing bipyridines failed. The bipyridine scaffold is essential for the B2Pin2 reduction system where B2Pin2 reduces Ni(II) to Ni(0) via reductive elimination of Bpin-X.

Title: Bis(alkyl) Cross-Coupling via Nickel Catalysis: Scope and Mechanistic Investigations
Reaction: primary alkyl bromides + primary alkyl iodides → unsymmetrical dialkyl products
Ligand Knowledge: Isolated [(bpp)Ni(I)Cl] was characterized (EPR, X-ray). bpp vs terpyridine give different selectivities, attributed to ligand-based redox events rather than metal-centered ones.

Title: Cross-Electrophile Coupling of Alkyl Halides with Nickel and Photoredox Catalysis
Reaction: alkyl halides + alkyl halides (intramolecular cyclization)

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Coupling of Aryl Halides with Allylic Carbonates
Reaction: aryl halides + allylic carbonates → aryl-allyl products
Ligand Knowledge: iPr-PyBox gave 81% for 2-substituted allylic carbonates vs dtbbpy at 26%. dtbbpy + pyridine-2,6-diamine was optimal for 3-substituted/unsubstituted substrates. The ligand controls allyl-Ni binding mode (η1 vs η3) and thus regioselectivity (>9:1 vs 2:1).

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Cross-Coupling of Aryl Halides with Alkyl Halides
Reaction: aryl halides + alkyl halides → aryl-alkyl cross-coupling products

Title: Ligand-Controlled Reductive Cross-Coupling of Aryl Halides with Alkyl Halides
Reaction: aryl bromides + alkyl iodides → aryl-alkyl cross-coupling products
Ligand Knowledge: Isolated [(dtbpy)Ni(Ar)I] is only competent when 4-CN-Py is added, confirming the monodentate ligand activates a dormant Ni(II) intermediate.

Title: Ketones from Nickel-Catalyzed Decarboxylative, Non-Symmetric Cross-Electrophile Coupling of Carboxylic Acid Esters
Reaction: NHP esters (alkyl radical donor via decarboxylation) + S-2-pyridyl thioesters (acyl donor) → aryl alkyl & dialkyl ketones
Ligand Knowledge: The electron-poor ligand balances oxidative addition of thioester and SET to NHP ester

Title: Visible-Light-Enabled Stereodivergent Synthesis of E- and Z-Configured 1,4-Dienes by Photoredox/Nickel Dual Catalysis
Reaction: allylic carbonates + vinyl triflates → E- or Z-configured 1,4-dienes (skipped dienes)

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Synthesis of Dialkyl Ketones from the Coupling of N-Alkyl Pyridinium Salts with Activated Carboxylic Acids
Optimum Ligand: 4,4',4''-tri-tert-butyl-2,2':6',2''-terpyridine [tBu-terpy] for primary alkyl pyridinium salts; Bathophenanthroline [BPhen] for secondary alkyl pyridinium salts
Reaction: N-alkyl pyridinium salts (alkyl radical donor via deaminative C-N cleavage) + acyl fluorides (primary) or 2-pyridyl esters (secondary) (acyl donors) → dialkyl ketones

Title: Diverse Synthesis of Chiral Trifluoromethylated Alkanes via Nickel-Catalyzed Asymmetric Reductive Cross-Coupling Fluoroalkylation
Reaction: CF3-containing alkyl bromides (racemic) + aryl iodides/bromides → chiral α-aryl-CF3 alkanes

Title: Radical 1,4-Aryl Migration Enabled Remote Cross-Electrophile Coupling of alpha-Amino-beta-Bromo Acid Esters with Aryl Bromides
Reaction: beta-bromo-α-benzylamino acid esters + aryl bromides → remote aryl-migrated/arylated amino acid ester products

Title: Chemoselective and Diastereoselective Synthesis of C-Aryl Nucleoside Analogues by Nickel-Catalyzed Cross-Coupling of Furanosyl Acetates with Aryl Iodides
Reaction: furanosyl acetates (protected D-ribofuranose derivatives) + aryl iodides (including dihalogenated and heteroaryl iodides) → C-aryl nucleoside analogues (beta-anomeric C-aryl furanosides)

Title: The Formal Cross-Coupling of Amines and Carboxylic Acids to Form sp3-sp3 Carbon-Carbon Bonds
Reaction: Katritzky salts (activated amines) + redox-active esters (from carboxylic acids) → C(sp3)-C(sp3) coupled products
Ligand Knowledge: The strongly electron-withdrawing CF3 groups on the bipyridine backbone are essential for promoting this deaminative-decarboxylative C(sp3)-C(sp3) cross-coupling.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Stereo- and Enantioselective Cross-Coupling of gem-Difluoroalkenes with Carbon Electrophiles by C–F Bond Activation
Reaction: gem-difluoroalkenes (bearing ester directing group) + racemic benzyl chlorides (and selected alkyl halides) → enantioenriched monofluoroalkenes with stereogenic allylic centers
Ligand Knowledge: The indanyl substituent is critical for achieving high stereocontrol during the chelation-assisted C–F bond oxidative addition step.

Title: Reductive Alkylation of Alkenyl Acetates with Alkyl Bromides by Nickel Catalysis
Optimum Ligand: 5,5'-dimethyl-2,2'-bipyridine [5,5'-CH3-bpy] as the primary ligand; 4,4'-di-tert-butyl-2,2'-bipyridine [4,4'-tBu-bpy] for specific substrates e.g., secondary/tertiary alkyl bromides
Reaction: alkenyl acetates (styryl, heteroaryl-substituted, cyclic enone-derived) + primary and secondary alkyl bromides → aliphatic alkenes

Title: From Esters to Ketones via a Photoredox-Assisted Reductive Acyl Cross-Coupling Strategy
Reaction: aryl/alkyl 2-pyridyl esters (acyl electrophiles) + N-(acyloxy)phthalimide esters (alkyl radical precursors) → ketones
Ligand Knowledge: The electron-withdrawing acetyl groups on the bipyridine motifs are key to suppressing side pathways.

Title: Control of Redox-Active Ester Reactivity Enables a General Cross-Electrophile Approach to Access Arylated Strained Rings
Reaction: strained-ring NHP esters (cyclopropane, bicyclo[1.1.1]pentane, bicyclo[2.2.2]octane, oxetane, azetidine, cyclobutane carboxylic acid-derived) + (hetero)aryl halides (iodides/bromides) → arylated strained-ring products containing all-carbon quaternary centers
Ligand Knowledge: 4,4'-tBu-BpyCamCN avoids problematic side reactions by better matching the catalyst's reactivity with the NHP ester radical generation rate.

Title: Direct Synthesis of alpha-Aryl-alpha-Trifluoromethyl Alcohols via Nickel Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Coupling
Reaction: N-trifluoroethoxyphthalimide (redox-active ester) + iodoarenes → α-aryl-α-trifluoromethyl alcohols
Ligand Knowledge: Electron-donating groups at 4,4' improve yields, while sterically hindering 6,6'-substituents derail selectivity toward homocoupling.

Title: A Photoinduced, Nickel-Catalyzed Reaction for the Stereoselective Assembly of C-Linked Glycosides and Glycopeptides
Reaction: glycosyl bromides/chlorides + NHPI esters (from aliphatic carboxylic acids) or pyridinium salts (from primary amines) → C-alkyl glycosides and glycopeptides

Title: Ligand-Controlled Nickel-Catalyzed Regiodivergent Cross-Electrophile Alkyl-Alkyl Couplings of Alkyl Halides
Optimum Ligand: 5,5'-dimethyl-2,2'-bipyridine [5,5'-CH3-bpy] for γ-coupling; 2,2'-(1,3-di(naphthalen-1-yl)propane-2,2-diyl)bis(4-(anthracen-9-ylmethyl)-4,5-dihydrooxazole) [specific BOX ligand] for β-coupling; 2,2'-(4-chloropyridine-2,6-diyl)bis(4,5-dihydrooxazole) [4-Cl-PyBox] for δ-ipso coupling
Reaction: δ-bromoamides (migratory alkyl bromides) + unactivated alkyl bromides → δ-, γ-, or beta-alkylated amides
Ligand Knowledge: Ligand choice directly dictates regioselectivity.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed C(sp2)-I-Selective C(sp2)-C(sp3) Cross-Electrophile Coupling of Bromo(iodo)arenes with Alkyl Bromides
Optimum Ligand: 4'-hydroxy-2,2':6',2''-terpyridine [4'-OH-terpy] for secondary and tertiary alkyl bromides; N-(pyridin-2-ylmethyl)-4-(trifluoromethyl)picolinamide [specific tridentate ligand] for 1 degree alkyl bromides
Reaction: bromo(iodo)arenes + alkyl bromides (3 degrees, 2 degrees, 1 degree) → arene-alkyl coupled products with C(sp2)-I selectivity
Ligand Knowledge: Tridentate ligands suppressed formation of the isomerization byproduct (3-iso), whereas bidentate ligands did not; more electron-rich terpyridine ligands delivered higher yields of the desired product.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Electrochemical Cross-Electrophile C(sp2)-C(sp3) Coupling via a NiII Aryl Amido Intermediate
Optimum Ligand: Bathophenanthroline [BPhen] as primary ligand + isoamylamine as secondary ligand
Reaction: (hetero)aryl halides (bromides, iodides, chlorides, triflates) + primary/secondary alkyl bromides (including cycloalkyl, branched, adamantyl) → (hetero)aryl-alkyl cross-coupling products
Ligand Knowledge: BPhen was identified as the best primary ligand among 10 polypyridine ligands screened. Without the isoamylamine secondary ligand, BPhen gave only 3% cross-product with 91% aryl homo-coupling; adding isoamylamine (3 equiv) boosted cross-product to 90% and suppressed homo-coupling to 6%.

Title: Metallaphotoredox-Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Couplings of Aryl Chlorides and Alkyl Halides: Harnessing the sigma-Donor/pi-Acceptor Synergy of a 2-(1H-Imidazol-2-yl)pyridine Ligand
Reaction: aryl chlorides + alkyl halides (bromides and iodides) → alkylated arenes
Ligand Knowledge: Phenyl substituent on pyridine motif facilitates superior ligand orientation in the metal periphery, enhancing oxidative addition to the challenging aryl chloride, allowing these PyIm ligands serve as “flexible” electron reservoirs capable of modulating and accommodating the necessary redox changes that occur in the catalytic cycle

Title: Picolinamide Ligands: Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Cross-Coupling of Aryl Bromides with Bromocyclopropane and Beyond
Reaction: (hetero)aryl bromides + bromocyclopropane (and other alkyl bromides) → arylcyclopropanes (and other alkylated arenes)
Ligand Knowledge: Congested rigid tridentate ligands inhibit oxidative addition of both partners, giving poor yields with debromination as the major pathway. The specific picolinamide ligand are congested but flexible pincer geometry suppresses aryl homocoupling while still permitting oxidative addition of bromocyclopropane.

Title: Ni-Catalyzed Reductive Allylation of Unactivated Alkyl Halides with Allylic Carbonates
Optimum Ligand: 2,6-bis(4-isopropyl-2-oxazolin-2-yl)pyridine [PyBoxiPr] for alkyl iodides; 4,4',4''-tri-tert-butyl-2,2':6',2''-terpyridine [tBu-terpy] for alkyl bromides; 2,2'-(4-methoxypyridine-2,6-diyl)bis(4,5-dihydrooxazole) [4-OCH3-PyBox] for challenging 3-alkyl, 2-aryl, and 2-alkyl allylic carbonates
Reaction: unactivated alkyl halides (cyclic and acyclic) + substituted allylic carbonates → allylated alkanes (exclusively E-alkenes)

Title: Expedient Synthesis of Chiral alpha-Amino Acids through Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Cross-Coupling
Reaction: serine/homoserine-derived iodides (α-iodo-amino acid derivatives) + aryl halides/acyl chlorides/alkyl halides → non-natural chiral α-amino acids

Title: Cross-Electrophile Coupling of Vinyl Halides with Alkyl Halides
Optimum Ligand: 2,2'-bipyridine [bipy], used as pre-formed NiI2(bpy); 4,4'-dimethoxy-2,2'-bipyridine [4,4'-OCH3-bpy] for (E)-vinyl halides
Reaction: (Z)- and (E)-vinyl bromides (and vinyl triflates) + primary and secondary alkyl halides → substituted alkenes
Ligand Knowledge: At the low catalyst loading (1 mol%) used in this study, the performance difference between bipy, 4,4'-tBu-bpy, and 4,4'-OCH3-bpy was not significant , unlike at higher loadings where 4,4'-tBu-bpy and 4,4'-OCH3-bpy had previously been shown to reduce dimer formation. Simple bipy was chosen as the primary ligand for practical advantages: it is the least expensive and the NiI2(bipy) pre-catalyst is air- and moisture-stable for months. For (E)-vinyl halides specifically, 4,4'-OCH3-bpy provided the highest yield.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Coupling with Organic Reductants in Non-Amide Solvents
Reaction: benzyl chlorides + alkyl iodides/aryl halides + diarylmethanes; aryl iodides/bromides + alkyl iodides → aryl-alkyl cross-coupling products

Title: A Practical Method for Continuous Production of sp3-Rich Compounds from (Hetero)Aryl Halides and Redox-Active Esters
Reaction: (hetero)aryl iodides/bromides + redox-active esters (N-hydroxyphthalimide esters) → C(sp3)-C(sp2) cross-coupled products

Title: Dumbbell-Shaped 2,2'-Bipyridines: Controlled Metal Monochelation and Application to Ni-Catalyzed Cross-Couplings
Optimum Ligand: dumbell shaped 5,5'-Ar-bpy ligands, with 3,5-disubstituted trityl groups at C5/C5' positions
Reaction: aryl bromides + alkyl bromides → aryl-alkyl cross-coupling products
Ligand Knowledge: dsbpys outperform conventional bpy ligands (bpy, 4,4'-tBu-bpy, 4,4'-OCH3-bpy, 5,5'-CH3-bpy, 5,5'-Mes-bpy) by enforcing monochelation to Ni. UV-vis titration confirmed L3-L5 selectively form [Ni(dsbpy)] monochelates, whereas conventional bpy ligands form bischelated [Ni(bpy)2] species. The bischelated species promote aryl homocoupling via bimolecular disproportionation of [Ar-Ni-Br]; monochelating dsbpys suppress this side reaction (aryl homocoupling drops from 31-69% with bpy-type ligands to 5-14% with dsbpys ligand).

Title: ChemBead Enabled High-Throughput Cross-Electrophile Coupling Reveals a New Complementary Ligand
Reaction: aryl bromides + alkyl bromides (including neopentyl, tertiary alkyl, beta-leaving group, and heteroaryl substrates) → C(sp2)-C(sp3) coupled products

Title: Nickel-Mediated Photoreductive Cross Coupling of Carboxylic Acid Derivatives for Ketone Synthesis
Reaction: N-acylsaccharins (acyl electrophiles derived from carboxylic acids) + NHP-esters (redox-active esters as alkyl radical precursors) → ketones
Ligand Knowledge: The rigid phenanthroline scaffold facilitated the key nickel redox steps more effectively than the more flexible bipyridine-based ligands in this photoredox-driven ketone synthesis.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Difluoroalkylation of (Hetero)aryl Bromides with Unactivated 1-Bromo-1,1-difluoroalkanes
Reaction: (hetero)aryl bromides + unactivated 1-bromo-1,1-difluoroalkanes → difluoroalkylated (hetero)arenes

Title: Enantioselective Reductive Cross-Coupling of Aryl/Alkenyl Bromides with Benzylic Chlorides via Photoredox/Biimidazoline Nickel Dual Catalysis
Reaction: aryl/alkenyl bromides + benzylic chlorides → chiral 1,1-diaryl alkanes and aryl allylic compounds
Ligand Knowledge: The authors note in the introduction that BiIm ligands were chosen over previously reported BiOx ligands because their more electron-rich character favors oxidative addition of C(sp2)–Br bonds.

Title: Stereoselective Synthesis of C-Vinyl Glycosides via Nickel-Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Couplings of 1,2-Glycosyl Orthoesters and Vinyl Halides
Reaction: 1,2-glycosyl orthoesters (glycosyl radical precursor) + vinyl halides → C-vinyl glycosides
Ligand Knowledge: Complete failure of dbbpy (bidentate) suggests tridentate coordination is essential for sterically congested glycosyl radical intermediates.

Title: alpha-Amino Radical Halogen Atom Transfer Agents for Metallaphotoredox-Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Couplings of Distinct Organic Halides
Reaction: organic bromides (aryl, heteroaryl, alkenyl, alkyl) + organic iodides (alkyl, aryl, alkenyl, alkynyl) → diverse C-C coupled products

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Cross-Coupling of alpha-Trifluoromethylated Alkyl Electrophiles and Allylic Sulfones
Reaction: α-trifluoromethyl alkyl bromide + allylic sulfone → α-trifluoromethyl homoallylic alkane

Title: Nickel-catalyzed reductive coupling of glucosyl halides with aryl/vinyl halides enabling beta-selective preparation of C-aryl/vinyl glucosides
Reaction: C1-glucosyl halide (glucosyl bromide or chloride) + aryl/vinyl iodide → beta-C-aryl/vinyl glucoside
Ligand Knowledge: The tridentate tBu-Terpy ligand controls diastereoselectivity by favoring beta-attack of the Ac-protected glucosyl radical on the Terpy-Ni(II)-Ar intermediate, as the bulky Terpy-Ni(II) intermediate sterically directs approach to the beta-face. In contrast, bidentate pyridine/DMAP ligands gave α-selectivity.

Title: Electrochemical Synthesis of Ketones from Acid Chlorides and Alkyl and Aryl Halides Catalysed by Nickel Complexes
Reaction: acid chlorides (acyl donor) + alkyl/aryl halides → unsymmetric ketones

Title: Ligand-promoted reductive coupling between aryl iodides and cyclic sulfonium salts by nickel catalysis
Reaction: aryl iodide + cyclic alkyl(aryl)sulfonium salt → aryl alkyl thioether (anti-Markovnikov selective ring-opening product)
Ligand Knowledge: The bulky terpyridine ligand with a p-dimethylaminophenyl substituent promotes anti-Markovnikov selectivity and suppresses homocoupling.

Title: Ni-catalyzed activation of α-chloroesters: a simple method for the synthesis of α-arylesters and β-hydroxyesters
Reaction: aryl halides (aryl iodides and bromides) + α-chloroesters (methyl 2-chloropropanoate, methyl 2-chloroacetate) → α-arylesters; and α-chloroesters + carbonyl compounds (ketones, aldehydes) → β-hydroxyesters (Reformatsky-type)

Title: A practical nickel-catalyzed reductive alkylation of amidophenyl bromides
Reaction: amidophenyl bromide (N-acetyl-3-bromophenylamine) + primary alkyl bromide → alkylated amidophenyl
Ligand Knowledge: Among 2,2'-dipyridyl ligands, less electron-rich variants performed better: 4,4'-OCH3-bpy gave trace yield, 4,4'-CH3-bpy gave 14%, 4-CH3-bpy gave 34%, and the unsubstituted 2,2'-bipyridine gave the best yield at 61%. Phosphine ligands and 1,10-phenanthroline failed. Decreasing electron-donating character of the dipyridyl ligand improved the coupling yield.

Title: An extension of nickel-catalyzed reductive coupling between tertiary alkyl halides with allylic carbonates
Optimum Ligand: 2,6-bis(4-isopropyl-2-oxazolin-2-yl)pyridine [PyBoxiPr] for allylic carbonates; 4,7-dimethoxy-2,9-dimethyl-1,10-phenanthroline [4,7-OCH3-2,9-CH3-phen] for dienyl carbonates
Reaction: chloro-cyclotryptamine analog (tertiary alkyl chloride) + allylic carbonate (method A) or dienyl methyl carbonate (method B) → allylated/dienylated pyrroloindoline

Title: Ni-Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Coupling of alpha-Hydroxy Carbonyl Compound-Derived Oxalates with Vinyl Triflates
Reaction: α-hydroxy carbonyl-derived oxalates (alkyl radical precursor via C-O fragmentation) + vinyl triflates → beta,γ-unsaturated carbonyl compounds

Title: Homogeneous organic reductant based on 4,4'-tBu2-2,2'-bipyridine for cross-electrophile coupling
Reaction: aryl halides + 1-bromo-3-phenylpropane → aryl-alkyl cross-coupled products

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Reductive Couplings of Neopentyl Bromides with Aryl Bromides
Reaction: aryl bromides + neopentyl-type alkyl bromides → alkylated arenes

Title: A Process Chemistry Benchmark for sp2-sp3 Cross Couplings
Optimum Ligand: 4,4′-di-tert-butyl-2,2′-bipyridine [4,4'-tBu-bpy] for alkyl bromide coupling (highest selectivity); 1,10-phenanthroline [Phen] selected for process due to cost; 4,4'-dimethoxy-2,2'-bipyridine [4,4'-OCH3-bpy] for alkyl tosylate coupling
Reaction: 5-bromo-1H-indole-2-carboxylic acid tert-butyl ester + N-Boc-4-bromopiperidine (or N-Boc-4-piperidinyl tosylate) → piperidine-containing indole fragment

Title: Desulfurative Ni-Catalyzed Reductive Cross-Coupling of Benzyl Mercaptans/Mercaptoacetates with Aryl Halides
Reaction: perfluoroaryl thioether (activated benzyl mercaptan) + aryl halide (aryl bromide or aryl iodide) → diarylmethane

Title: Nickel-Mediated Synthesis of Non-Anomeric C-Acyl Glycosides through Electron Donor Acceptor Complex Photoactivation
Reaction: glycosyl carboxylic acids (pyranosyl/furanosyl acids activated in situ with DMDC) + alkyl redox-active esters (NHP esters) → non-anomeric C-acyl glycosides

Title: Nickel/Photoredox-Catalyzed Enantioselective Reductive Cross-Coupling between Vinyl Bromides and Benzyl Chlorides
Reaction: vinyl bromides + benzyl chlorides → enantioenriched products bearing allylic stereogenic centers

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Arylation of Redox Active Esters for the Synthesis of alpha-Aryl Nitriles: Investigation of a Chlorosilane Additive
Reaction: NHP ester (derived from cyanoacetic acid) + aryl iodide → α-aryl nitrile

Title: Engaging Nonaromatic, Heterocyclic Tosylates in Reductive Cross-Coupling with Aryl and Heteroaryl Bromides
Reaction: nonaromatic heterocyclic tosylate (e.g., N-Boc-piperidin-4-yl tosylate) + aryl/heteroaryl bromide → sp3-sp2 cross-coupled product

Title: Coupling of Challenging Heteroaryl Halides with Alkyl Halides via Nickel-Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Coupling
Optimum Ligand: N-cyano-2-pyridylcarboxamidine [PyCamCN], optimal for 4-halopyridines; pyridine-2,6-bis(carboximidamide) [PyBCam] and N2,N6-dicyanopyridine-2,6-bis(carboximidamide) [PyBCamCN] optimal for 3-bromopyridines; 2-pyridylcarboxamidine [PyCam], optimal for 2-bromopyridines; ligand pyridine-2,6-bis(carboximidamide) [PyBCam] identified as most general
Reaction: heteroaryl halides (2-, 3-, 4-pyridyl halides, triazolopyridinyl, naphthyridinyl, pyrazolopyridinyl halides) + primary/secondary alkyl halides → alkylated heterocycles
Ligand Knowledge: The optimal ligand depends on the heteroaryl halide substitution pattern.Bipyridine ligands provided low yields for more complex heterocycles due to competing hydrodehalogenation and dimerization, whereas the N-cyanocarboxamidine ligands suppressed these side reactions.

Title: Development and Execution of an Ni(II)-Catalyzed Reductive Cross-Coupling of Substituted 2-Chloropyridine and Ethyl 3-Chloropropanoate
Reaction: substituted 2-chloropyridine + ethyl 3-chloropropanoate → 2-alkylpyridine

Title: New Ligand Development for Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Cross-Coupling Enabling Practical Synthesis of SGLT-2 Inhibitors
Reaction: 2,3,4,6-tetra-O-acetyl-α-D-glucopyranosyl bromide + aryl iodides → C-aryl beta-D-glucosides (SGLT-2 inhibitor intermediates)

Title: N-Silyl Radical Mediated Cross-Electrophile Coupling of Acyl-imides with Alkyl Bromides under Photoredox/Nickel Dual Catalysis
Reaction: N-acyl imide + unactivated alkyl bromide → alkyl ketone

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Cross-Coupling of Alkyl Carboxylic Acid Derivatives with Pyridinium Salts via C-N Bond Cleavage
Reaction: carboxylic acid derivative (acid chloride or carboxylic acid) + alkylpyridinium salt → ketone

Title: Allylboronates from Vinyl Triflates and alpha-Chloroboronates by Reductive Nickel Catalysis
Optimum Ligand: 2,2'-bipyridine [bipy] for cyclic vinyl triflates; Bathophenanthroline [BPhen] for acyclic vinyl triflates
Reaction: α-chloroboronates (alkyl radical precursor) + vinyl triflates (vinyl electrophile) → allylboronates

Title: Ni-Catalyzed Reductive Coupling of Aryl Bromides with Alkyl Bromides
Reaction: aryl bromides + primary/secondary alkyl bromides → aryl-alkyl cross-coupling products
Ligand Knowledge: dtbbpy combined with 1 equiv pyridine as additive is the key to achieving high yields. Pyridine promotes aryl bromide oxidative addition at room temperature. The tridentate Cl-PyBox ligand alone gave only 32% yield; the bidentate + monodentate N/N combinatorial strategy is essential.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Directed Cross-Electrophile Coupling of Phenolic Esters with Alkyl Bromides
Reaction: o-hydroxyphenyl ester (phenolic ester) + primary/secondary alkyl bromide → o-hydroxyl aryl ketone

Title: Using Chlorotrifluoroethane for Trifluoroethylation of (Hetero)aryl Bromides and Chlorides via Nickel Catalysis
Reaction: CF3CH2Cl (chlorotrifluoroethane) + (hetero)aryl bromides and chlorides → trifluoroethylated (hetero)arenes

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Vinylation of Chloro-hexahydropyrroloindoline Derivatives with Vinyl Triflates
Reaction: chloro-hexahydropyrroloindoline derivatives (tertiary alkyl radical precursor) + vinyl triflates (vinyl electrophile) → C-3 vinyl substituted hexahydropyrrolidinoindolines
Ligand Knowledge: Preformed Ni(bpy)Br2 (10 mol%) gave 95% NMR yield (91% isolated). When Ni and bipyridine were added separately (NiBr2 + 10% bpy), yield dropped to 85%, suggesting the preformed complex provides a more effective catalytic species.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Diastereoselective Reductive Cross-Coupling of Disubstituted Cycloalkyl Iodides with Aryl Iodides
Reaction: disubstituted cycloalkyl iodide (e.g., tert-butyl((2-iodocyclohexyl)oxy)dimethylsilane) + aryl iodide (e.g., 4-iodobenzonitrile) → trans-2-arylcycloalkyl product

Title: Deaminative Reductive Methylation of Alkylpyridinium Salts
Reaction: secondary Katritzky alkylpyridinium salts + methyl iodide → C(sp3)-methylated alkyl products

Title: Alkenylation and Arylation of Peptides via Ni-Catalyzed Reductive Coupling of α-C-Tosyl Peptides with Csp2 Triflates/Halides
Reaction: α-C-tosyl peptides (di- and tripeptides) + vinyl triflates/bromides + aryl iodides → vinyl/aryl-modified peptides

Title: Photoredox/Nickel Dual Catalysis Enables the Synthesis of Alkyl Cyclopropanes via C(sp3)-C(sp3) Cross Electrophile Coupling of Unactivated Alkyl Electrophiles
Reaction: 1,3-dialkyl electrophile (1,3-dimesylate or 1,3-dibromide, intramolecular) → alkyl cyclopropane

Title: Ni-Catalyzed Asymmetric Reductive Alkenylation of alpha-Chlorosulfones with Vinyl Bromides
Reaction: α-chlorosulfones (C(sp3) electrophile, radical precursor) + vinyl bromides (C(sp2) electrophile) → chiral allylic sulfones

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed C(sp3)-C(sp3) Cross-Electrophile Coupling of In Situ Generated NHP Esters with Unactivated Alkyl Bromides
Reaction: NHP ester (in situ generated from carboxylic acid) + unactivated alkyl bromide → 1-degree/1-degree C(sp3)-C(sp3) coupled product

Title: Synthesis of Aryl Secondary Amides via Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Coupling of Redox-Active Esters
Reaction: NHP esters of malonic acid half amides + aryl iodides → α-aryl secondary amides

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Coupling of γ-Metalated Ketones with Unactivated Alkyl Bromides
Optimum Ligand: 2,2′-bipyridine (bipy); 5,5′-dimethyl-2,2′-dipyridine (5,5'-CH3-bpy) for functionalized alkyl bromides bearing ether/chloride
Reaction: Aryl cyclopropyl ketones + unactivated alkyl bromides → γ-alkyl-substituted ketones

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed, Manganese-Assisted Denitrogenative Cross-Electrophile Coupling of Benzotriazinones with Alkyl Halides for ortho-Alkylated Benzamides
Reaction: Benzotriazinones + alkyl halides (Cl, Br, I) → ortho-alkylated secondary benzamides

Title: Ni-Catalyzed Reductive Cross-Coupling of Cyclopropylamines and Other Strained Ring NHP Esters with (Hetero)Aryl Halides
Reaction: Cyclopropylamine/strained ring NHP esters + (hetero)aryl iodides/bromides → 1-arylcyclopropylamines
Ligand Knowledge: Only one ligand system (bipyridine) was employed throughout this study, as part of the preformed dinuclear nickel precatalyst [(bipy)2Ni2(μ-Cl)2Cl2(H2O)2] precatalyst. The reaction achieved 85% yield with 5 mol % of [(bipy)2Ni2(μ-Cl)2Cl2(H2O)2] precatalyst at 0 °C for aryl iodides. For more challenging aryl bromides, the same ligand system worked at room temperature. The oxetane substrate required NiCl2(dtbbpy) at 20 mol % loading, indicating that a more electron-rich bipyridine was needed for that particular substrate class.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Decarboxylative/Deaminative Glycosylation of Activated Aliphatic Acids and Primary Amines
Reaction: Redox-active esters/Katritzky’s N-alkylpyridinium salts + 1-iodo-glycals → alkyl C-glycosides
Ligand Knowledge: For the decarboxylative glycosylation, NiBr2(DME) with bpp gave 63% yield. Ligand screening revealed 4,4'-tBu-bpy was optimal at 82%. For the deaminative glycosylation 2,2′-bipyridine proved optimal at 75% with NiBr2(DME) and 85% isolated with NiCl2(DME). In both reaction types, the ligand is essential, with 4,4'-tBu-bpy and 2,2′-bipyridine providing the best balance of electronic and steric properties for stabilizing the Ni intermediates involved in radical capture and reductive elimination with the glycal substrate.

Title: Electrochemical Nickel-Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Coupling of Alkenyl Triflates with alpha-Chloroamides
Reaction: α-chloroamides (alkyl radical precursor) + alkenyl triflates (alkenyl electrophile) → α-functionalized amide derivatives

Title: Synthesis of Stereodefined Enones from the Cross-Electrophile Coupling of Activated Acrylic Acids with Alkyl Bromides
Reaction: Acrylic acid 2-pyridyl esters + alkyl bromides → (E)-trisubstituted enones
Ligand Knowledge: bipy gave 87% GC yield (84% isolated) with >99:1 E/Z selectivity. The electron-rich ligand 4,4'-OCH3-bpy consumed alkyl bromide faster than the 2-pyridyl ester, yielding 77% with >99:1 E/Z. The electron-poor ligand 4,4'-CO2Me-bpy favored an unselective ketonization side product, giving only 32% yield with 88:1 E/Z and leaving 32% unconsumed alkyl bromide. Without any ligand, product still formed at 27% yield with 24:1 E/Z. The electron-neutral bipy optimally balanced the consumption rates of both electrophilic coupling partners, achieving the highest yield and stereoselectivity while suppressing ketonization and E/Z isomerization side reactions.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Cross-Coupling of (Hetero)Aryl Iodides with Fluorinated Secondary Alkyl Bromides
Reaction: Fluorinated secondary alkyl bromides (CF3(Alkyl)CHBr) + (hetero)aryl iodides → fluoroalkyl-substituted tertiary carbon products

Title: Translation of Nickel-Catalyzed C(sp2)–C(sp3) Cross-Electrophile Coupling to Non-Amide Solvents
Optimum Ligand: 4,4′-bis(dimethylamino)-2,2′-bipyridine [4,4'-NMe2-bpy]; 4,4′-dimethoxy-2,2'-bipyridine [4,4'-OCH3-bpy] for electron-rich aryl bromides; picolinimidamide [PyCamH] for secondary alkyl bromides
Reaction: Aryl bromides + alkyl bromides → aryl-alkyl cross-coupling products
Ligand Knowledge: In MeCN as solvent, Tthe strongly electron-donating NMe2 substituents provided significant selectivity improvements over alternative ligands for the desired cross-coupled product over aryl homodimer, consistent with previous studies. For coupling electron-rich aryl bromides, 4,4'-OCH3-bpy was employed as ligand for higher conversion. For secondary alkyl bromides, PyCam substantially improved yields over bipyridine ligands, demonstrating that the LiI/picoline solvent-flexibility system is not limited to bipyridine ligands. DMAP (4-dimethylaminopyridine) as a pyridine additive was detrimental, giving only 28% yield, likely because its strong donating ability displaces the bipyridine ligand from nickel.

Title: Ester Formation via Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Coupling of Alkyl Halides with Chloroformates
Reaction: Alkyl iodides + chloroformates → alkyl esters
Ligand Knowledge: The complex (L)NiII-(O)CR are stable in DMF, while (L)NiII-(O)COR is not, to circumvent this problem the small amount of ligand and precatalyst is used.

Title: Stereoselective Preparation of alpha-C-Vinyl/Aryl Glycosides via Nickel-Catalyzed Reductive Coupling of Glycosyl Halides with Vinyl and Aryl Halides
Optimum Ligand: Pyridine [Py] for vinylation; 4-Dimethylaminopyridine [DMAP] for arylation with electron-poor aryl iodides; 4,4'-di-tert-butyl-2,2'-bipyridine [4,4'-tBu-bpy] for arylation with electron-rich aryl iodides
Reaction: glycosyl halides (C(sp3) electrophile) + vinyl bromides or aryl iodides (C(sp2) electrophile) → α-C-vinyl/aryl glycosides
Ligand Knowledge: The labile pyridine ligand uniquely delivers high α-selectivity through dissociation upon α-face approach. For arylation: DMAP optimal for electron-poor aryl iodides; dtbBipy needed for electron-rich ones but at the expense of α-selectivity.

Title: Deaminative Reductive Cross-Electrophile Couplings of Alkylpyridinium Salts and Aryl Bromides
Reaction: Alkylpyridinium salts + aryl bromides → alkyl-aryl cross-coupling products
Ligand Knowledge: The electron-rich 4,4'-OCH3-bpy outperformed the less electron-donating 4,4′-tBu-bpy because the increased electron density facilitates single-electron transfer to the pyridinium cation, which is critical for generating the alkyl radical intermediate needed for productive cross-coupling rather than formation of the dihydropyridine side product.

Title: Ni-Catalyzed Reductive Deaminative Arylation at sp3 Carbon Centers
Reaction: Alkylpyridinium salts + aryl bromides → alkyl-aryl cross-coupling products
Ligand Knowledge: The 4,4′-donor substituents on the bipyridine ligand enhance electron density at the nickel center, facilitating the interception of in situ generated alkyl radicals (formed via SET from Mn to the pyridinium salt) by the Ni–aryl intermediate, which is critical for productive C(sp2)–C(sp3) bond formation via the proposed Ni-catalyzed radical capture/reductive elimination pathway.

Title: Ni-Catalyzed Cross-Electrophile Coupling for the Synthesis of Skipped Polyenes
Reaction: allyl trifluoroacetates (allyl electrophile) + vinyl bromides (vinyl electrophile) → skipped polyenes (1,4-dienes, 1,4,7-trienes)
Ligand Knowledge: bipy (10-20 mol%) was the standard ligand; no systematic ligand screening. 4,4'-OCH3-bpy was used instead to minimize hydrolysis of the trifluoroacetate electrophile, demonstrating ligand-substrate matching for sensitive substrates.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Decarboxylative Alkenylation of Anhydrides with Vinyl Triflates or Halides
Optimum Ligand: 1,10-phenanthroline (phen) for vinyl triflates; 2,2'-bipyridine (bipy) for vinyl bromides/iodides
Reaction: Aliphatic acid anhydrides + vinyl triflates or vinyl halides → alkenylated products
Ligand Knowledge: The less sterically hindered and electron-neutral 1,10-phenanthroline performed best among the tested ligands for vinyl triflates. For vinyl bromides and iodides, replacing 1,10-phenanthroline with 2,2′-bipyridine and raising the temperature to 25 °C gave the best results. The authors attribute the superior performance of 1,10-phenanthroline to its lower steric profile and electron-neutral character, which facilitates the decarboxylative coupling with vinyl triflates under mild conditions.

Title: Desulfonylative Arylation of Redox-Active Alkyl Sulfones with Aryl Bromides
Reaction: Alkyl phenyl tetrazole sulfones + aryl bromides → arylated (fluoro)alkyl products

Title: Reductive sp3–sp2 Coupling Reactions Enable Late-Stage Modification of Pharmaceuticals
Reaction: 5-Chloroaminopyrimidine kinase inhibitor (aryl bromide) + primary/secondary alkyl halides → alkylated pharmaceutical derivatives
Ligand Knowledge: A high-throughput screen of 12 ligands across two solvents (DMA and MeCN) revealed that only tBu-terpy and iPr-PyBox in acetonitrile afforded any substantial product (>5%). This was notable because imine ligands are typically effective in C(sp2)-C(sp2) reductive couplings with simple heterocyclic and alkyl halides, yet they failed here. Although iPr-PyBox showed slightly superior product selectivity, tBu-terpy was selected for scope evaluation due to its achirality and greater commercial accessibility. The failure of common ligands like dtbbpy and phenanthrolines with this nitrogen-rich, sterically hindered pharmaceutical substrate underscores the critical importance of ligand screening for complex molecule cross-coupling, where the terpyridine scaffold apparently provides the necessary coordination environment to handle Lewis basic functionality on the aryl partner.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Deaminative Acylation of Activated Aliphatic Amines with Aromatic Amides via C–N Bond Activation
Reaction: Katritzky salts (N-alkylpyridinium salts) + activated aromatic amides (N-acylsuccinimides) → ketones
Ligand Knowledge: The paper notes that suitable electron density on the ligand was crucial for the coupling reaction. 1,10-Phenanthroline with its planar, rigid bidentate structure and appropriate electron density clearly outperformed all other nitrogen ligands tested.

Title: Nickel-Catalyzed Direct Acylation of Aryl and Alkyl Bromides with Acylimidazoles
Optimum Ligand: 2,2′-bipyridine [bipy] for alkyl-aryl ketones; 2,2':6',2''-terpyridine [terpy] for aryl-aryl ketones; 4,4′-dimethoxy-2,2′-bipyridine (4,4'-OCH3-bpy) for alkyl-alkyl ketones
Reaction: Acylimidazoles + aryl/alkyl bromides → ketones (alkyl-aryl, aryl-aryl, alkyl-alkyl)
Ligand Knowledge: Three distinct optimal ligands were identified for different coupling regimes. Condition A uses unmodified 2,2'-bipyridine ligand with NiI2, Zn, and ZnBr2 for alkyl-aryl ketone formation; each component including the ligand was required for high yield.
